question:
Players $1,2,3,\ldots,n$ are seated around a table, and each has
a single penny.  Player 1 passes a penny to player 2, who then passes
two pennies to player 3.  Player 3 then passes one penny to Player 4,
who passes two pennies to Player 5, and so on, players alternately
passing one penny or two to the next player who still has some
pennies.  A player who runs out of pennies drops out of the game and
leaves the table.  Find an infinite set of numbers $n$ for which some
player ends up with all $n$ pennies.
solution:
We show more precisely that the game terminates with one player
holding all of the pennies if and only if $n = 2^m + 1$ or $n = 2^m +
2$ for some $m$. First suppose we are in the following situation for
some $k \geq 2$. (Note: for us, a ``move'' consists of two turns,
starting with a one-penny pass.)
\begin{itemize}
\item
Except for the player to move, each player has $k$ pennies;
\item
The player to move has at least $k$ pennies.
\end{itemize}
We claim then that
the game terminates if and only if the number of players is a
power of 2. First suppose the number of players is even; then after
$m$ complete rounds, every other player, starting with the player who
moved first, will have $m$ more pennies than initially, and the others
will all have 0. Thus we are reduced to the situation with half as
many players; by this process, we eventually reduce to the case where
the number of players is odd. However, if there is more than one
player, after two complete rounds everyone has as many pennies as they
did before (here we need $m \geq 2$), so the game fails to terminate.
This verifies the claim.

Returning to the original game, note that after one complete round,
$\lfloor \frac{n-1}{2} \rfloor$ players remain, each with 2 pennies
except for the player to move, who has
either 3 or 4 pennies. Thus by the above argument, the game terminates
if and only if $\lfloor \frac{n-1}{2} \rfloor$ is a power of 2, that
is, if and only if $n = 2^m + 1$ or $n = 2^m + 2$ for some $m$.

descriptive_long.question: Players $1,2,3,\ldots,playercount$ are seated around a table, and each has a single penny.  Player 1 passes a penny to player 2, who then passes two pennies to player 3.  Player 3 then passes one penny to Player 4, who passes two pennies to Player 5, and so on, players alternately passing one penny or two to the next player who still has some pennies.  A player who runs out of pennies drops out of the game and leaves the table.  Find an infinite set of numbers $playercount$ for which some player ends up with all $playercount$ pennies.
descriptive_long.solution:
We show more precisely that the game terminates with one player holding all of the pennies if and only if $playercount = 2^{roundindex} + 1$ or $playercount = 2^{roundindex} + 2$ for some $roundindex$. First suppose we are in the following situation for some $pennyquota \geq 2$. (Note: for us, a ``move'' consists of two turns, starting with a one-penny pass.)
\begin{itemize}
\item
Except for the player to move, each player has $pennyquota$ pennies;
\item
The player to move has at least $pennyquota$ pennies.
\end{itemize}
We claim then that
the game terminates if and only if the number of players is a
power of 2. First suppose the number of players is even; then after $roundindex$ complete rounds, every other player, starting with the player who
moved first, will have $roundindex$ more pennies than initially, and the others
will all have 0. Thus we are reduced to the situation with half as
many players; by this process, we eventually reduce to the case where
the number of players is odd. However, if there is more than one
player, after two complete rounds everyone has as many pennies as they
did before (here we need $roundindex \geq 2$), so the game fails to terminate.
This verifies the claim.

Returning to the original game, note that after one complete round,
$\lfloor \frac{playercount-1}{2} \rfloor$ players remain, each with 2 pennies
except for the player to move, who has
either 3 or 4 pennies. Thus by the above argument, the game terminates
if and only if $\lfloor \frac{playercount-1}{2} \rfloor$ is a power of 2, that
is, if and only if $playercount = 2^{roundindex} + 1$ or $playercount = 2^{roundindex} + 2$ for some $roundindex$.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $n\ge 3$ be an integer and identify the vertices of a regular
$n$-gon with the cyclic group  
\[
C_{n}=\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z
      =\{\overline 0,\overline 1,\ldots ,\overline{n-1}\},\qquad
      \overline j+\overline k:=\overline{\,j+k\,}.
\]

Every vertex $\overline j\in C_{n}$ is initially occupied by  

\hspace*{1.9em}$\bullet$ one {\em active contestant},\qquad
\hspace*{1.9em}$\bullet$ one identical {\em jade gem}.  

Fix a vertex $A\in C_{n}$; the contestant placed there is called the
{\em pointer}.  
As long as at least three contestants are still active the following  

\begin{center}
{\bf basic move}
\end{center}

is executed.

1.\; {\bf Choice of the acting triple.}\;
   Clockwise from the pointer take the first three {\em distinct}
   active contestants  
   \[
      S_{0},\;S_{1},\;S_{2},\qquad S_{0}=\text{current pointer};
   \]

2.\; {\bf Gem transfers.}\;
   $S_{0}$ gives {\em one} gem to $S_{1}$; immediately afterwards
   $S_{1}$ gives {\em two} gems to $S_{2}$;

3.\; {\bf Retirements.}\;
   Every contestant whose stock has just dropped to $0$ leaves the game
   forever;

4.\; {\bf Pointer update.}\;
   The new pointer is the {\em first} active contestant met strictly
   clockwise {\em after} $S_{2}$.

The game stops automatically as soon as fewer than three contestants are
active.  
If, at that moment, exactly one contestant is still active we say that
the game {\em terminates successfully}.

(a)\; For which integers $n\ge 3$ does there exist {\em at least one}
    initial pointer $A$ for which the game terminates successfully?
    For every such $n$ exhibit an explicit winning pointer.

(b)\; For which integers $n\ge 3$ does the game terminate successfully
    {\em for every} choice of the initial pointer?
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  
\[
n=3k+r,\qquad 0\le r\le 2,\qquad k=\Bigl\lfloor\frac n3\Bigr\rfloor .
\tag{1}
\]

A contestant owning exactly one gem is called {\em light}, otherwise
{\em heavy}.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
1.\;  An initial deterministic sweep
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
Index the contestants clockwise by  
\[
P_{0}:=\text{pointer},\;P_{1},\ldots ,P_{n-1}.
\]

Because everybody is light, the first move acts on
$(P_{0},P_{1},P_{2})$, eliminates $P_{0},P_{1}$ and turns $P_{2}$ into
a heavy with $3$ gems.  
The new pointer is $P_{3}$.  
Repeating this argument $k$ times yields the

\medskip\noindent
{\bf Canonical configuration}

\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] $H:=\{P_{2},P_{5},\ldots ,P_{3k-1}\}$ consists of $k$
           heavies, each of them owning $3$ gems;
\item[(ii)] if $r=2$ the two lights are $L_{0}:=P_{3k}$ (pointer) and
            $L_{1}:=P_{3k+1}$, whereas for $r\in\{0,1\}$ no light is
            present;
\item[(iii)] the configuration obtained so far is uniquely determined
             by $n$ and does {\em not} depend on the original pointer.
\end{enumerate}

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
2.\;  The residue $r=1$ - impossibility
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
Assume $r=1$, i.e.\ $n=3k+1\;(k\ge 1)$.  
The single light $L:=P_{3k}$ is the pointer.  
The next move eliminates $L$ and leaves $k$ heavies, all owning at
least $2$ gems.  

\medskip\noindent
{\bf Lemma 1.}\;
If every active player owns at least $2$ gems, then {\em exactly} two
players retire in each subsequent move; consequently the number of
active contestants can never drop from $\ge 3$ to $1$.

\smallskip
{\em Proof.}\;
Let $(S_{0},S_{1},S_{2})$ be the acting triple.
$S_{2}$ gains two gems and therefore survives.
A player retires precisely when he started the move with $1$ gem.
If $S_{0}$ retires, then $S_{1}$ has also lost one gem and could not
have owned more than $2$ before the move; hence $S_{1}$ retires as
well, and conversely.  
\hfill$\square$

\medskip
After the elimination of $L$ at least $k\ge 1$ players are active.
Lemma~1 now forbids a successful termination, so

\[
\boxed{\;r=1\ \Longrightarrow\ \text{no success is possible.}\;}
\tag{2}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
3.\;  Reduction to a heavy-only situation for $r\in\{0,2\}$
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\underline{$r=0$.}\;
The canonical configuration is already heavy-only.

\smallskip
\underline{$r=2$.}\;
Only the lights $L_{0}$ (pointer) and $L_{1}$ are not heavy.  
The unique legal move
\[
S_{0}=L_{0},\quad S_{1}=L_{1},\quad S_{2}=P_{2}
\]
eliminates $L_{0},L_{1}$ and raises the stock of $P_{2}$ from $3$ to
$5$ gems.  

\smallskip
Combining both cases, the game has now reached a position with  

\[
k\ge 1\quad\text{active contestants }Q_{0},Q_{1},\ldots ,Q_{k-1}
\ ( \text{clockwise order}), \qquad
g(Q_{j})\ge 3\;(j\ne 0),\;g(Q_{0})\in\{3,5\},
\tag{3}
\]
where $Q_{0}$ is the pointer and the indices are taken modulo $k$.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
4.\;  Dynamical decomposition into colour classes
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
Set 
\[
d:=\gcd(3,k),\qquad k=3d\ell\ ( \ell\ge 1).
\tag{4}
\]

Because in every move the pointer jumps {\em three} steps clockwise,
its index modulo $d$ never changes.
Hence the $k$ active contestants split into $d$ {\em colour classes}

\[
C_{s}:=\{Q_{3t+s}\mid 0\le t\le 3\ell-1\},\qquad 0\le s\le d-1,
\]
each of size $3\ell$.  

During one {\em block} of $3\ell$ consecutive moves every contestant
appears once as starter, once as middle and once as receiver, whence
his net gem change in this block equals $-1-1+2=0$.  
Consequently

\begin{quote}
{\em if $d=1$ no retirement is ever possible.}
\end{quote}

In view of Lemma~1 and (2) this leaves only

\[
d=3\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad 3\mid k
\tag{5}
\]
as a candidate for a successful game.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
5.\;  The critical case $d=3$
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
Write $k=3\ell$ and enumerate
\[
C_{0}:=\{Q_{3t}\},\quad
C_{1}:=\{Q_{3t+1}\},\quad
C_{2}:=\{Q_{3t+2}\}\quad(0\le t\le \ell-1).
\]

\medskip\noindent
{\bf Lemma 2.}\;
During one block of $\ell$ consecutive moves the {\em individual}
changes read  
\[
\Delta(C_{0})=-1,\qquad \Delta(C_{1})=-1,\qquad \Delta(C_{2})=+2 .
\tag{6}
\]

\smallskip
{\em Proof.}\;
Within such a block every member of $C_{0}$ (resp.\,$C_{1},C_{2}$) is
starter (resp.\,middle, receiver) exactly once.
\hfill$\square$

\bigskip
Repeated application of (6) shows that the gem differences
{\em inside one colour class} never change.  
Denote by
\[
m:=\min_{0\le j\le k-1} g(Q_{j})
\tag{7}
\]
the current minimal stock.

\medskip\noindent
{\bf Lemma 3 (simultaneous disappearance of two colour classes).}\;
Suppose $m>0$ at some moment and let $B$ be the unique block during
which $m$ becomes $0$.  
Then at the {\em end} of $B$ every member of $C_{0}\cup C_{1}$ has
retired, whereas all members of $C_{2}$ survive.

\smallskip
{\em Proof.}\;
Because each contestant of $C_{0}\cup C_{1}$ loses exactly one gem per
block, they all reach $0$ simultaneously in $B$.  
Every member of $C_{2}$ gains two gems in that same block, hence none
of them can retire there.
\hfill$\square$

\medskip
After the block $B$ only the $\ell$ contestants of $C_{2}$ remain.
They sit pairwise separated by two empty seats, but for the ongoing
play we may contract every arc of three consecutive positions into one
single vertex.
In this {\em contracted polygon} the survivors are consecutive, the
pointer is still $Q_{0}$, and the rules of the game are unchanged.
Therefore the whole situation has reproduced itself with  

\[
k\;\longleftarrow\;\ell=\frac k3 ,
\qquad\text{while all survivors possess at least }3\text{ gems.}
\tag{8}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
6.\;  Final classification
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
Iterating the contraction (8) we obtain a non-increasing sequence  

\[
k_{0}:=k,\qquad
k_{j+1}:=\begin{cases}
\dfrac{k_{j}}3,& 3\mid k_{j},\\[6pt]
k_{j},& 3\nmid k_{j},
\end{cases}
\tag{9}
\]
which stabilises at some $k_{\infty}\in\{1,2\}$.

\medskip\noindent
{\bf Lemma 4.}\;
$k_{\infty}=1$ if and only if $k$ is a power of $3$.

\smallskip
{\em Proof.}\;
If $k=3^{m}$, then $k_{j}=3^{m-j}$ until $j=m$, whence
$k_{\infty}=1$.  
Conversely, if $k_{\infty}=1$, every division in (9) is exact; thus
$k=3^{m}$.
\hfill$\square$

\bigskip
{\em (i)  $k_{\infty}=2$.}\;
After the last contraction two contestants remain, so the game has
already stopped {\em unsuccessfully}.

\smallskip
{\em (ii) $k_{\infty}=1$.}\;
At the last stage $k=1$, hence exactly one contestant is active and
the game terminates {\em successfully}.

\medskip
Collecting (2), (5) and Lemma~4 we have shown that

\[
\boxed{\;
\begin{aligned}
\text{a successful termination is \emph{possible}}\;\Longleftrightarrow\;&
r\in\{0,2\}\ \text{and}\ k=3^{m}\ (m\ge 0).
\end{aligned}}
\tag{10}
\]

Because $n=3k+r$, condition (10) amounts to

\[
\boxed{\;
n\in\{\,3^{m},\,3^{m}+2\mid m\ge 0\}.
\;}
\tag{11}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
7.\;  Winning and universal pointers
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
(a)\; {\bf Existence.}\;
For every $n$ listed in (11) choose the initial pointer $A$ so that,
after the deterministic sweep of Section~1, the contestant with
possibly $5$ gems belongs to the {\em receiver} class $C_{2}$
(this is always possible by a rotation of all indices).
With that choice the proof above shows that the game ends in a single
survivor.  

Explicitly, if $n=3^{m}+2$ write $n=3k+2$ with $k=3^{m-1}$ ($m\ge 1$)
and take  
\[
A=\overline{\,3k+1\,};
\qquad
\text{for }n=5\;(m=0)\text{ take }A=\overline 0.
\]

(b)\; {\bf Universality.}\;
If $n=3^{m}$ ($r=0$ and $k=3^{m-1}$) then {\em every} initial pointer
yields the same heavy-only configuration (all heavies possess $3$
gems), so the preceding argument works verbatim; hence the game
terminates successfully {\em for every} choice of the pointer.

If $n=3^{m}+2$ ($m\ge 0$) the pointer chosen in (a) is winning, but
placing the pointer two steps clockwise produces a heavy with $5$
gems inside the starter class $C_{0}$; in that case the first
contraction already leaves two survivors, so the game fails.
Thus successful termination is {\em not} universal.

\[
\boxed{\;
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Successful for \emph{every} pointer } \Longleftrightarrow
      n=3^{m}\;(m\ge 0),\\
&\text{Successful for \emph{some}   pointer } \Longleftrightarrow
      n=3^{m}\ \text{or}\ n=3^{m}+2\;(m\ge 0).
\end{aligned}}
\]

\hfill$\square$

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• The original problem dealt with one fixed ordering of the players.
  The enhanced variant allows an *arbitrary* step size r coprime to n,
  so the effective order of play can be any of the φr–orbits of the
  cyclic group.  One must therefore recognise and exploit an abstract
  group isomorphism to reduce the general situation to a single model
  case.  This demands comfort with cyclic groups and permutation
  conjugacy – concepts absent from the original statement.

• Besides total‐gem conservation (the only invariant used in the
  current kernel) we introduced and had to track a subtler invariant,
  the alternating sum modulo 3, to rule out spurious possibilities.
  Proving and using such a congruence invariant is a level of
  sophistication not needed in the easier setting.

• The solution requires blending three techniques:
  (i)  a relabelling argument using group theory,
  (ii) a round-wise induction reducing n via a non-trivial recursive
       formula, and
  (iii) an invariant-based impossibility proof.
  The original problem needs only (ii); the current kernel variant
  uses essentially (ii) with light congruence folklore, whereas the
  enhanced version forces the solver to coordinate all three strands.

• Conceptually, the solver must see through the apparent additional
  freedom (the parameter r) and prove that it *does not* enlarge the
  set of solvable n – a non-obvious assertion that sharply raises the
  cognitive load compared with merely finding one infinite family.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $n\ge 3$ be an integer and set  

\[
C_{n}= \mathbf Z / n\mathbf Z
      =\{\overline 0,\overline 1,\dots ,\overline{n-1}\},
      \qquad
      \overline j+\overline k:=\overline{\,j+k\,}.
\]

Every vertex $\overline j\in C_{n}$ of the regular $n$-gon is occupied by  

\hspace*{1.6em}$\bullet$ one {\it active contestant},  

\hspace*{1.6em}$\bullet$ one identical {\it jade gem}.  

Choose one vertex $A\in C_{n}$; the contestant stationed there is called the
{\it pointer}.
While at least three contestants remain active the
following

\begin{center}
{\bf basic move}
\end{center}
is executed.

1.\quad {\it Choice of the acting triple.}  
   Starting with the pointer and moving clockwise, take the first three
   {\it distinct} active contestants  

   \[
        S_{0},\;S_{1},\;S_{2},\qquad S_{0}= \text{ current pointer};
   \]

2.\quad {\it Gem transfers.}  

   $S_{0}$ gives {\it one} gem to $S_{1}$; immediately afterwards
   $S_{1}$ gives {\it two} gems to $S_{2}$;

3.\quad {\it Retirements.}  

   Every contestant whose stock has just fallen to $0$ gems leaves the
   game for good;

4.\quad {\it Pointer update.}  

   The new pointer is the {\it first} active contestant met strictly
   clockwise {\em after} $S_{2}$.\smallskip

The game stops automatically as soon as fewer than three contestants are
active.  
If exactly {\it one} contestant is still active at that moment we say
that the game {\it terminates successfully}.

(a)\; For which integers $n\ge 3$ does there exist
    {\em at least one} initial pointer $A$ for which the game
    terminates successfully?
    For every such $n$ exhibit an explicit winning pointer.

(b)\; For which integers $n\ge 3$ does the game terminate successfully
    {\em for every} choice of the initial pointer?



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we write  

\[
n=3k+r,\qquad 0\le r\le 2 ,\qquad k=\bigl\lfloor\tfrac n3\bigr\rfloor ,
\]
and call a contestant  

\[
\textit{light}\;:\;1\text{ gem},\qquad
\textit{heavy}\;:\;\ge 2\text{ gems}.
\]
All gem numbers are non-negative integers.



------------------------------------------------------------------
1.\;  A deterministic first sweep
------------------------------------------------------------------

Fix the clockwise enumeration  

\[
\boxed{P_{0}:=\text{initial pointer},\;P_{1},\dots ,P_{n-1}}
\tag{1.1}
\]

of the vertices.  
Because initially every contestant is light, the first move involves the
triple $(P_{0},P_{1},P_{2})$; both $P_{0}$ and $P_{1}$ retire,
whereas $P_{2}$ turns heavy with $3$ gems and the pointer jumps to
$P_{3}$.  
Repeating the same reasoning $k$ times we arrive at

\smallskip
{\bf Claim 1.}  
After exactly $k$ basic moves the position is

\begin{itemize}
\item[(a)] the $2k$ contestants  

      \[
      P_{0},P_{1},P_{3},P_{4},\dots ,P_{3k-3},P_{3k-2}
      \]

      have retired;

\item[(b)] the $k$ vertices  

      \[
      H:=\{P_{2},P_{5},\dots ,P_{3k-1}\}
      \]

      survive, each with {\it exactly $3$ gems};

\item[(c)] the lights and the pointer are

\[
\begin{array}{rcl}
r=0 &:& \text{no light, pointer }=P_{2};\\[2pt]
r=1 &:& \text{one light }L:=P_{3k},\;\text{pointer }=L;\\[2pt]
r=2 &:& \text{two consecutive lights }L_{1}:=P_{3k},\,L_{2}:=P_{3k+1},
          \text{ pointer }=L_{1}.
\end{array}
\]
\end{itemize}



------------------------------------------------------------------
2.\;  A monotone alternating-sum invariant
------------------------------------------------------------------

Whenever {\it all} active contestants are heavy
we label them clockwise  

\[
Q_{0},Q_{1},\dots ,Q_{m-1}\qquad (m\ge 2),
\]

choosing the current pointer to be $Q_{0}$.  
Define the alternating sum  

\[
\Lambda=\sum_{j=0}^{m-1} (-1)^{j}\,g(Q_{j}),
\tag{2.1}
\]

where $g(Q_{j})$ denotes the number of gems presently owned by $Q_{j}$.

\medskip\noindent
{\bf Lemma 2.1 (strict drift of $\Lambda$).}
While only heavies are active one has {\it at every move}

\[
\Delta\Lambda = +2 .
\]

\emph{Proof.}
Let $S_{0}=Q_{0},\;S_{1}=Q_{1},\;S_{2}=Q_{2}$ be the acting triple and
write

\[
g(Q_{0})=x,\quad g(Q_{1})=y,\quad g(Q_{2})=z\qquad (x,y,z\ge 2).
\]

During the move the three stocks change by  

\[
(x,y,z)\longmapsto(x-1,\;y-1,\;z+2).
\]

Hence

\[
\Delta\Lambda
= (+1)\cdot(-1)+(-1)\cdot(-1)+(+1)\cdot(+2)=+2 .
\tag{2.2}
\]

After the transfers the pointer becomes $Q_{3}$, so every index is
reduced by $3$.  
Because $(-1)^{j-3}=(-1)^{j}$ the signs in
\eqref{2.1} stay unchanged; therefore
\eqref{2.2} already gives the {\it total} increment of $\Lambda$ for
one full move.  \hfill$\square$

\smallskip
Consequently $\Lambda$ increases by the fixed amount $2$ every turn but
is certainly bounded above by the total number of gems:

\[
\Lambda\le n.
\tag{2.3}
\]

Therefore {\it a heavy-only position cannot persist for ever}; after at
most $\lceil n/2\rceil$ further moves at least one active contestant
must have dropped to $1$ gem, i.e. a light is created.



------------------------------------------------------------------
3.\;  Reduction by two contestants
------------------------------------------------------------------

Assume that a light is present and denote by $T_{0},T_{1},T_{2}$ the
three residue classes modulo $3$ with respect to the current pointer
(index $0$).  
In every move the changes in those three classes are

\[
T_{0}:\, -1,\qquad T_{1}:\, -1,\qquad T_{2}:\, +2 .
\tag{3.1}
\]

Hence lights can occur only in $T_{0}$ or $T_{1}$.
Two different situations have to be distinguished.

\smallskip
{\bf (i) A light belongs to $T_{0}$ (the pointer).}  
It passes its sole gem and retires immediately; the
middle player $S_{1}\in T_{1}$ decreases her stock by $1$ as well and
therefore becomes {\it light}.  
Thus the number of actives has decreased by {\it exactly two}.
If at that moment at least three contestants remain, we are again in
the setting of (i) or (ii) below.

\smallskip
{\bf (ii) A light belongs to $T_{1}$.}
During the move it first receives a gem (becoming heavy) and then has
to pay two gems, so it retires, while the starter in $T_{0}$ loses one
gem and turns light.
Again we have eliminated precisely two contestants and are back to (i).

\smallskip
In either case a light triggers the simultaneous retirement of
{\it two} consecutive players and afterwards the play continues with an
{\it odd} number of active contestants (because an even number has just
been removed).



------------------------------------------------------------------
4.\;  Even versus odd number of heavies
------------------------------------------------------------------

\smallskip
{\bf Lemma 4.1 (even number of heavies - impossibility).}
If all active contestants are heavy and their number $m$ is {\it even},
then the game can never terminate successfully.

\emph{Proof.}
Label the heavies as in Section 2 and mark
$Q_{0},Q_{2},\dots ,Q_{m-2}$.  
Each move alters the numbers of marked and unmarked gems
respectively by $(-1)+2=+1$ and $-1$ (if the starter is marked) or the
opposite (if he is unmarked), so the difference
$\Gamma=$(marked gems)$-$(unmarked gems) always changes by $\pm2$.
Because $m$ is even, the parity of the starter alternates, whence
$\lvert\Gamma\rvert$ stays bounded by $n/2+1<n$ for $n\ge 6$.  
A lone winner, however, would give $\lvert\Gamma\rvert=n$, a
contradiction.  (For $m=2$ a new move is already impossible.)
\hfill$\square$

\smallskip
{\bf Lemma 4.2 (odd number of heavies - convergence).}
Suppose that at some moment all active contestants are heavy and their
number $m$ is {\it odd}.  
Then, independently of the current pointer, the play terminates
successfully after finitely many further moves.

\emph{Proof.}
By Lemma 2.1 the alternating sum $\Lambda$ rises by $2$ each turn until
a light appears, which must happen before $\Lambda$ exceeds $n$.
Lemma 4.1 implies $m\ge 3$; hence when the first light is formed
Section 3 eliminates two active players and leaves an {\it odd} number
$m-2$ of heavies.  
Induction on odd $m$ completes the proof; the induction step does not
use any property of the pointer, so the conclusion is pointer-independent.
\hfill$\square$



------------------------------------------------------------------
5.\;  The three residue classes of $n$
------------------------------------------------------------------

(a)\; $r=1$ ($n=3k+1$).  
Claim 1 produces $k$ heavies and one light $L$ with the pointer sitting
at $L$.  
We are in case 3(i): the very next move eliminates $L$ and {\it one}
heavy, leaving an {\it even} number $k-1$ of heavies.
Lemma 4.1 then forbids a single winner - hence no pointer wins if
$n\equiv1\pmod3$.

\smallskip
(b)\; $r=0$ ($n=3k$).  
After the first sweep only the $k$ heavies in $H$ survive.

\hspace*{1.8em}$\bullet$ If $k$ is even ($n\equiv0\pmod6$) Lemma 4.1
excludes success.

\hspace*{1.8em}$\bullet$ If $k$ is odd ($n\equiv3\pmod6$) Lemma 4.2
yields termination and, thanks to pointer-independence, every pointer
is winning.

\smallskip
(c)\; $r=2$ ($n=3k+2$).  
Claim 1 leaves $k$ heavies ($k\ge1$) and two lights
$L_{1},L_{2}$ with the pointer at $L_{1}$.
One additional move (case 3(i)) eliminates both lights,
creates an extra $2$ gems on $P_{2}$ and places the pointer on $P_{5}$.
Hence we again have $k$ heavies.

\hspace*{1.8em}$\bullet$ If $k$ is even ($n\equiv2\pmod6$) Lemma 4.1
rules out success.

\hspace*{1.8em}$\bullet$ If $k$ is odd ($n\equiv5\pmod6$) Lemma 4.2
applies, so the game ends successfully, and it does so independently of
the initial pointer.

\smallskip
Combining (a), (b) and (c) we obtain

\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a) There exists a winning pointer } &\Longleftrightarrow
& n\equiv 3,\,5 \pmod 6;\\[4pt]
\text{(b) Every pointer is winning } &\Longleftrightarrow
& n\equiv 3,\,5 \pmod 6 .
\end{aligned}}
\]

If $n\equiv3 \pmod6$ the victory is pointer-independent because the game
reaches a heavy-only configuration with an odd number of contestants.
For $n\equiv5\pmod6$ (including $n=5$) exactly one preparatory move is
required before that same situation is attained, so again {\it every}
initial pointer is winning.

\medskip
{\bf Small cases $n=3,4,5$.}

\[
\begin{array}{lcl}
n=3: &\text{Exactly one basic move is possible and leaves a lone
      survivor.}&(\text{all pointers win})\\[2pt]
n=4: &\text{The only move leaves two active contestants.}
      &(\text{termination impossible})\\[2pt]
n=5: &\text{Two moves remove four players and heap all
      gems on the fifth.}&(\text{all pointers win})
\end{array}
\]

These special values fit the boxed classification.



\bigskip
This completes the corrected solution.



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• The original problem dealt with one fixed ordering of the players.
  The enhanced variant allows an *arbitrary* step size r coprime to n,
  so the effective order of play can be any of the φr–orbits of the
  cyclic group.  One must therefore recognise and exploit an abstract
  group isomorphism to reduce the general situation to a single model
  case.  This demands comfort with cyclic groups and permutation
  conjugacy – concepts absent from the original statement.

• Besides total‐gem conservation (the only invariant used in the
  current kernel) we introduced and had to track a subtler invariant,
  the alternating sum modulo 3, to rule out spurious possibilities.
  Proving and using such a congruence invariant is a level of
  sophistication not needed in the easier setting.

• The solution requires blending three techniques:
  (i)  a relabelling argument using group theory,
  (ii) a round-wise induction reducing n via a non-trivial recursive
       formula, and
  (iii) an invariant-based impossibility proof.
  The original problem needs only (ii); the current kernel variant
  uses essentially (ii) with light congruence folklore, whereas the
  enhanced version forces the solver to coordinate all three strands.

• Conceptually, the solver must see through the apparent additional
  freedom (the parameter r) and prove that it *does not* enlarge the
  set of solvable n – a non-obvious assertion that sharply raises the
  cognitive load compared with merely finding one infinite family.
